{{nihongo||流鏑馬|'''Yabusame'''}} is a type of {{ill|Mounted archery|en|Mounted archery|WD=Q1074665|lt=Mounted archery|1=Mounted archery}} in traditional [[Kyūdō|Japanese archery]]. An archer on a running horse shoots three special "turnip-headed" {{ill|Arrow|en|Arrow|WD=Q45922|lt=Arrow|1=Arrow}}s successively at three wooden targets.
This style of archery has its origins at the beginning of the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}. [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] became alarmed at the lack of archery skills his samurai possessed. He organized yabusame as a form of practice.
Nowadays, the best places to see yabusame performed are at the [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}} and [[Shimogamo Shrine]] in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} (during [[Aoi Matsuri]] in early May). It is also performed in {{ill|Samukawa|en|Samukawa|WD=Q1342787|lt=Samukawa|1=Samukawa}} and on the beach at [[Zushi]], as well as other locations.
== History == [[File:Yabusame in Sumida Park, Tokyo, -20 apr. 2013 a.webm|thumb|Yabusame in Sumida Park, Tokyo, 2013]] {{ill|Yumi|en|Yumi|WD=Q918499|lt=Japanese bows|1=Yumi}} date back to prehistoric times – the [[Jōmon period]]. The long, unique asymmetrical bow style with the grip below the center emerged under the [[:en:Yayoi|Yayoi]] culture (300 BC – 300 AD). Bows became the symbol of authority and power. The legendary first emperor of Japan, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, is always depicted carrying a bow.
Some {{ill|Emishi|en|Emishi|WD=Q932574|lt=Emishi|1=Emishi}} tribes, notably the Hitakami tribe, practice horse archery and were noticed and feared by the Yamato court.<ref>Aston, W.G., trans. Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to AD 697. Tokyo: Charles E.Tuttle Co., 1972 (reprint of two volume 1924 edition).
Takahashi, Tomio. ''"Hitakami.''" In Egami, Namio ed. ''Ainu to Kodai Nippon''. Tokyo: Shogakukan, 1982.</ref>
The use of the bow had been on foot until around the 4th century when elite soldiers took to fighting on horseback with bows and swords. In the 10th century, {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} would have archery duels on horseback. They would ride at each other and try to shoot at least three arrows. These duels did not necessarily have to end in death, as long as honor was satisfied.
One of the most celebrated incidents of Japanese mounted archery occurred during the {{ill|Genpei War|en|Genpei War|WD=Q737542|lt=Genpei War|1=Genpei War}} (1180–1185), an epic struggle for power between the [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] and [[Taira clan|Taira]] clans that was to have a major impact on Japanese culture, society, and politics.
At the {{ill|Battle of Yashima|en|Battle of Yashima|WD=Q2117322|lt=Battle of Yashima|1=Battle of Yashima}}, the Heike ([[Taira clan|Taira]]), having been defeated in battle, fled to Yashima and took to their boats. They were fiercely pursued by the Genji ([[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]]) on horseback, but the Genji were halted by the sea.
As the Heike waited for the winds to be right, they presented a fan hung from a mast as a target for any Genji archer to shoot at in a gesture of chivalrous rivalry between enemies.
One of the Genji samurai, {{ill|Nasu no Yoichi|en|Nasu no Yoichi|WD=Q2625800|lt=Nasu no Yoichi|1=Nasu no Yoichi}}, accepted the challenge. He rode his horse into the sea and shot the fan cleanly through. This feat is still celebrated to this day.
During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} (1192–1334), mounted archery was used as a military training exercise to keep samurai prepared for war. Those archers who did poorly might find themselves commanded to commit {{ill|Seppuku|en|Seppuku|WD=Q133462|lt=Seppuku|1=Seppuku}}, or ritual suicide.
One style of mounted archery was {{ill|Inuoumono|en|Inuoumono|WD=Q6059325|lt=Inuoumono|1=Inuoumono}} – shooting at dogs.<ref>Doris G. Bargen. ''Suicidal honor: General Nogi and the writings of Mori Ōgai and Natsume Sōseki''. University of Hawaii Press, 2006. {{ISBN|0-8248-2998-0}}, {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2998-8}}. [https://books.google.com/books?id=riI6iDtJGboC&dq=inuoumono&pg=PA107 Pg 107]</ref> Buddhist priests were able to prevail upon the samurai to have the arrows padded so that the dogs were only annoyed and bruised rather than killed. This sport is no longer practised.
==Ritual== [[Image:Samurai on horseback.png|thumb|A mounted {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} with bow and arrows, wearing a horned helmet. Circa 1878.]]
Yabusame was designed as a way to please and entertain the myriad of [[Kami|gods]] that watch over Japan, thus encouraging their blessings for the prosperity of the land, the people, and the harvest.
A yabusame archer gallops down a {{convert|255|m|yd|-1|adj=mid|-long}} track at high speed. The archer mainly controls his horse with his knees, as he needs both hands to draw and shoot his bow. As he approaches a target, he brings his bow up and draws the arrow past his ear before letting the arrow fly with a deep shout of In-Yo-In-Yo (darkness and light). The arrow is blunt and round-shaped in order to make a louder sound when it strikes the board.
Experienced archers are allowed to use arrows with a V-shaped prong. If the board is struck, it will splinter with a confetti-like material and fall to the ground. To hit all three targets is considered an admirable accomplishment. Yabusame targets and their placement are designed to ritually replicate the optimum target for a lethal blow on an opponent wearing full traditional samurai armor (O-Yoroi) which left the space just beneath the helmet visor bare.
Yabusame is characterized as a ritual rather than a sport because of its solemn style and religious aspects, and is often performed for special ceremonies or official events, such as entertaining foreign dignitaries and heads of state. Yabusame demonstrations have been given for the formal visits of US Presidents {{ill|Ronald Reagan|en|Ronald Reagan|WD=Q9960|lt=Ronald Reagan|1=Ronald Reagan}}, {{ill|George W. Bush|en|George W. Bush|WD=Q207|lt=George W. Bush|1=George W. Bush}}, and {{ill|Barack Obama|en|Barack Obama|WD=Q76|lt=Barack Obama|1=Barack Obama}}. A yabusame demonstration was given in the United Kingdom for [[:en:Prince Charles|Prince Charles]], who reportedly was fascinated and pleased with the performance.
To be selected as a yabusame archer is a great honor. In the past, they were chosen from only the best warriors. The archer who performs the best is awarded a white cloth, signifying divine favor.
==Schools== [[Image:Yabusame 04.jpg|thumb|Yabusame archer wearing traditional 13th century clothing]]
There are two schools of mounted archery that perform yabusame. One is the {{ill|Ogasawara-ryū|en|Ogasawara-ryū|WD=Q7079824|lt=Ogasawara school|1=Ogasawara-ryū}}. The founder, {{ill|Ogasawara Nagakiyo|en|Ogasawara Nagakiyo|WD=Q7079829|lt=Ogasawara Nagakiyo|1=Ogasawara Nagakiyo}}, was instructed by the ''shōgun'' [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] (1147–1199) to start a school for archery. Yoritomo wanted his warriors to be highly skilled and disciplined. Archery was seen as a good way for instilling the necessary principles for a samurai warrior.
{{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen|1=Zen}} became a major element in both foot and mounted archery as it also became popular among the samurai in every aspect of their life during the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}.
Yabusame as a martial art helped a samurai learn concentration, discipline, and refinement. Zen taught breathing techniques to stabilize the mind and body, giving clarity and focus. To be able to calmly draw one's bow, aim, and shoot in the heat of battle, and then repeat, was the mark of a true {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} who had mastered his training and his fear.
The other archery school was begun earlier by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiari|en|Minamoto no Yoshiari|WD=Q11564289|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiari|1=Minamoto no Yoshiari}} in the 9th century at the command of {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|Emperor Uda|WD=Q349267|lt=Emperor Uda|1=Emperor Uda}}. This school became known as the Takeda school of archery. The Takeda style has been featured in classic samurai films such as Akira Kurosawa's ''{{ill|Seven Samurai|en|Seven Samurai|WD=Q189540|lt=Seven Samurai|1=Seven Samurai}}'' (1954) and ''{{ill|Kagemusha|en|Kagemusha|WD=Q854990|lt=Kagemusha|1=Kagemusha}}'' (1980). {{ill|Toshiro Mifune|en|Toshiro Mifune|WD=Q37001|lt=Toshiro Mifune|1=Toshiro Mifune}}, was a noted student of the Takeda school.
==Decline and revival== [[File:Yabusame 05.jpg|thumb|Yabusame demonstrated for United States president {{ill|George W. Bush|en|George W. Bush|WD=Q207|lt=George W. Bush|1=George W. Bush}} (at the [[Meiji Jingu]] shrine)]]
With the arrival of the {{ill|Portugal|en|Portugal|WD=Q45|lt=Portuguese|1=Portugal}} and their guns in the mid-16th century, the bow began to lose its importance on the battlefield. At the {{ill|Battle of Nagashino|en|Battle of Nagashino|WD=Q428369|lt=Battle of Nagashino|1=Battle of Nagashino}} in 1575, well-placed groups of musketeers serving {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} and Tokugawa fired in volleys and practically annihilated the cavalry charges of the Takeda clan.
Mounted archery was revived in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} (1600–1867) by {{ill|Ogasawara Heibei Tsuneharu|en|Ogasawara Heibei Tsuneharu|WD=Q7079826|lt=Ogasawara Heibei Tsuneharu|1=Ogasawara Heibei Tsuneharu}} (1666–1747) under the command of the shogun {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|en|Tokugawa Yoshimune|WD=Q319664|lt=Tokugawa Yoshimune|1=Tokugawa Yoshimune}} (1684–1751). Given that the nation was at peace, archery as well as other military martial arts became more of a method of personal development rather than military training.
== Contemporary practice == [[File:Kamakura Yabusame.jpg|thumb|Yabusame at [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]]]] Yabusame is held at various times of the year, generally near [[Shinto]] shrines. On the 2nd Sunday of April every year, there is a Yabusame ceremony held at the Washibara Hachiman-gū shrine in {{ill|Tsuwano, Shimane|en|Tsuwano, Shimane|WD=Q640586|lt=Tsuwano, Shimane|1=Tsuwano, Shimane}}. At this ceremony, the Ogasawara school performs Yabusame at the oldest Yabusame Horse Archery range in Japan. In May, the [[Aoi Matsuri]] (Hollyhock festival) in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} includes yabusame.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/raku/backnum/may07_e/buttone3.html |title=Aoi matsuri |publisher= Kyoto City Tourism and Culture Information System}}</ref> Other locations include [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}}, together with {{ill|Samukawa|en|Samukawa|WD=Q1342787|lt=Samukawa|1=Samukawa}} and on the beach at [[Zushi]].
==Popular culture== * The {{ill|Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles (2003 TV series)|en|Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles (2003 TV series)|WD=Q158869|lt=2012 version of ''Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles''|1=Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles (2003 TV series)}} features yabusame being used by Gennosuke, the {{ill|Indian rhinoceros|en|Indian rhinoceros|WD=Q47910|lt=rhino|1=Indian rhinoceros}} {{ill|bounty hunter|en|bounty hunter|WD=Q621613|lt=bounty hunter|1=Bounty hunter}} and ally for the {{ill|Rabbit|en|Rabbit|WD=Q9394|lt=Rabbit|1=Rabbit}} samurai {{ill|Miyamoto Usagi|en|Miyamoto Usagi|WD=Q16170148|lt=Miyamoto Usagi|1=Miyamoto Usagi}}. * Yabusame was featured as part of the fighting styles of samurai members in ''{{ill|The Last Samurai|en|The Last Samurai|WD=Q207130|lt=The Last Samurai|1=The Last Samurai}}''. * ''{{ill|Power Rangers Samurai|en|Power Rangers Samurai|WD=Q848992|lt=Power Rangers Samurai|1=Power Rangers Samurai}}'' features yabusame being used by Blue Ranger [[List of Power Rangers Samurai characters#Kevin Douglas|Kevin Douglas]] uses his Hydro Bow while on horseback. * Yabusame is a minigame in [[:en:Rise of the Ronin|Rise of the Ronin]].
